retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Parotid Gland -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: Sympathetic and parasympathetic fibers innervate the parotid gland. Sympathetic innervation causes vasoconstriction, and parasympathetic innervation from the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX) produces the secretion of saliva. [5] The parasympathetic fibers originate in the inferior salivatory nucleus in the medulla and travel through the jugular foramen to the inferior ganglion. A small branch of the glossopharyngeal nerve called the tympanic or Jacobsen nerve, forms the tympanic plexus within the middle ear. These preganglionic fibers become the lesser petrosal nerve and course through the middle cranial fossa before exiting through the foramen ovale. They synapse in the otic ganglion, and the postganglionic parasympathetic fibers join the auriculotemporal nerve to innervate the parotid gland to secrete saliva.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Salivary Glands -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Identifying the facial nerve is essential when excising and resecting the parotid and submandibular gland to prevent further complications [23] . In the case of parotidectomies, the goal of the surgery is centered on carefully removing the tumor and the associated tissue margins of the gland and protecting the function of the facial nerve. Some anatomical landmarks for identifying the facial nerve are more effective than others. For example, the posterior digastric muscle and the tragal pointer are less reliable due to their variability or difficulty to localize. By contrast, bony landmarks are the most dependable and safest, such as the tympanomastoid fissure. Other methods for localizing the nerve trunk include retrograde tracing of its distal branches proximally [23] .

retrieved_docs.title: Parotidectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The parotid duct (Stenson duct) exits the gland anteriorly and follows a course approximately 1 cm inferior to and parallel to the zygomatic arch. After crossing the masseter, the duct pierces the buccinator muscle and enters the oral cavity, facing the second maxillary molar. Salivary secretions of the parotid gland are more serous in contrast with the submandibular gland, which secretes a more mixed serous/mucous saliva. This explains the higher incidence of sialolithiasis within the submandibular gland. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Parotid Gland -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Salivary Glands -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Parotid Gland
retrieved_docs.content: Blood from the maxillary vein and the superficial temporal vein join together to form the retromandibular vein, which runs through the parotid anterior to the facial nerve to join the external jugular vein. The retromandibular vein displays significant variation in its anatomy, and in some cases, splits into an anterior and posterior branch. The anterior branch can form a union with the posterior facial vein, forming the common facial vein. The posterior facial vein lies directly inferior to the marginal mandibular branch of the facial nerve and is often used as a landmark for pinpointing the nerve branch. By contrast, the posterior branch of the retromandibular vein can join with the post-auricular vein to drain into the external jugular vein [14] .

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lymph Nodes -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Level VIII: Parotid Group
retrieved_docs.content: Associated primary malignancies These nodes often contain metastatic deposits from malignancies of the previously named draining structures and the orbit, external auditory canal, and parotid gland. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lymph Nodes -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Level VIII: Parotid Group
retrieved_docs.content: Drainage These nodes drain the efferent lymphatics from the frontal and temporal skin, eyelids, conjunctivae, auricles, external acoustic meatus, tympanum, nasal cavities, the root of the nose, nasopharynx, and the eustachian tube.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Salivary Glands -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Parotid Gland
retrieved_docs.content: The external carotid artery provides the blood supply to the parotid gland, which flows superiorly from the carotid bifurcation alongside the posterior aspect of the digastric muscle. This artery courses medially to the parotid, bifurcating into the superficial temporal artery, which runs superior to the parotid to the scalp, and the maxillary artery, which travels from the medial side of the parotid to carry blood to the infratemporal fossa and the pterygopalatine fossa. Lastly, the transverse facial artery branches off the superficial temporal artery, projecting anteriorly between the zygoma and parotid duct to provide blood to both the parotid gland and parotid duct, as well as the masseter muscle.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Salivation -- Issues of Concern -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The parotid glands are located on each side of the face anterior to the ears and are made up of two lobes, a superficial lobe, and a deep lobe. Between these two lobes, lies the facial nerve which is important for the function of the muscles of facial expression. The duct of the parotid gland, also termed the Stensen duct, runs across the masseter muscle and exits into the oral cavity at the level of the upper second molar. The parotid gland is supplied by the external carotid artery and its branches and drained by the external jugular vein.

retrieved_docs.title: Parotidectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The parotid gland is the largest major salivary gland. This gland is bounded by the masseter muscle anteriorly, the zygomatic arch superiorly, the anteromedial aspect of the sternocleidomastoid muscle inferiorly, and the external auditory canal posteriorly. The facial nerve divides the gland virtually into superficial and deep lobes. The superficial lobe is lateral; the facial nerve and the deep lobe extend medially into the parapharyngeal space. However, this division is not anatomical since no true embryologic or fascial plane exists between the pars superficialis and pars profunda. The parotid gland is encased by a prolongation of the deep cervical fascia, which divides into a deep and superficial layer to surround the gland. The superficial fascia extends from the masseter and sternocleidomastoid to the zygomatic arch. The superficial musculoaponeurotic system (SMAS) extends superiorly from the platysma to the superficial temporal fascia. The SMAS lies superficial to the parotid fascia. [1] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Salivary Glands -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Parotid Gland
retrieved_docs.content: The lymphatic system of the parotid gland differs from the other major salivary glands in that there is a high density of lymph nodes in and around it. In this way, it contains two nodal layers, draining into both the superficial and deep cervical lymph systems. Roughly 90% of the nodes are found in the superficial layer between the glandular tissue and capsule [15] .

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Parotid Gland -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The parotid main excretory duct (Stensen duct) projects from the anterior portion of the superficial lobe and runs over the masseter muscle until it reaches its anterior border, from where it turns medially to penetrate the buccinator muscle [1] . This opens into the buccal cavity at the level of the buccal mucosa of the maxillary second molar. [1] The salivary glands share the same histological structure—a secretory portion called acini and a web of arborized ducts that open into the buccal cavity realizing saliva. [2] The parotid is a serous gland composed mainly of serous acinar cells, but it may contain accessory glandular tissue formed by mucinous acinar cells. Therefore, saliva excreted by the parotid is serous and watery. Each serous acinus is surrounded by myoepithelial cells that contract to help expel secretions from the acini. Furthermore, an extracellular matrix, stromal cells, immune cells, myofibroblasts, and nerves are found in the periphery of the acini. [4] Saliva is first produced in the acinar lumen and then altered into a mixture of electrolytes and macromolecules as it is actively transported through the ducts. The saliva is hypotonic when it reaches the mouth, but salivary flow rates can influence the electrolyte composition. In addition to electrolytes, saliva also contains mucin and digestive enzymes. The most important enzyme is amylase, which initiates the digestion of carbohydrates. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Salivation -- Issues of Concern -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of the salivary glands consists of a series of ducts that eventually end in either a spherical or tubular secretory acini or end piece. The main excretory duct is the one that empties into the oral cavity and divides into progressively smaller ductal units, the interlobar and interlobular excretory ducts. The main intralobular duct is the striated duct which is the main modifier of primary saliva. The striated ducts are connected to the secretory end pieces by intercalated ducts that branch several times before they join each end piece. The end piece and intercalated duct lumens are continuous. Some of the glands will contain intercellular canaliculi that extend to almost the level of the base of secretory cells as a means to increase the surface area of the cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Parotid Gland -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The parotid gland is the largest of the 3 paired major salivary glands, including the submandibular and sublingual glands; this gland is located in the retromandibular fossa, a space mainly occupied by this gland and is bordered superiorly by the zygomatic arch, anteriorly by the masseter muscle, and posteriorly by the sternocleidomastoid muscle. However, the superficial lobe extends anteriorly, covering the mandibular ramus and the posterior area of the masseter muscle. [1] See Images. The Mouth, Right Parotid Gland, Posterior, and The Mouth, Right Parotid Gland, anterior. The parotid gland and the other salivary glands play an essential function in the oral cavity because they secret saliva, facilitating chewing, swallowing, speaking, and digesting. [2] The facial nerve courses through the body of the parotid gland, creating a unique relationship between them, which requires focused attention when performing parotidectomies or other surgery in the region. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Parotid Gland -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The parotid gland is in intimate relation with the lymph nodes (see Image. Lymph Nodes of the Head and Neck). This is the only salivary gland with 2 nodal layers, which drain into the superficial and deep cervical lymph system. Most nodes are located within the superficial lobe between the gland and the parotid capsule. [2] Skin cancers of the face or scalp can enlarge the lymph nodes in the parotid gland, indicating the regional spread of carcinoma. Lymph nodes located in the parotid substance drain the gland, middle ear, nasopharynx, palate, and external meatus; the superficial preauricular lymph nodes drain the anterior pinna, temporal scalp, eyelids, and lacrimal glands. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lymph Nodes -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Level II: Upper Jugular Group
retrieved_docs.content: Drainage This group drains the efferent lymphatics of the face, parotid gland, level Ia, level Ib, and retropharyngeal nodes. It receives direct drainage from the nasal cavity, the entire pharyngeal axis, the larynx, the external auditory canal, the middle ear, and the sublingual and submandibular glands.

In addition, Excision is linked to other therapeutic interventions on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, indicating that it

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1271
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical concept that encompasses a wide range of operative techniques across multiple organ systems. It includes arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from a joint, exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the supratentorial region, suboccipital craniectomy for exploration or decompression of cranial nerves, transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration, cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty (with or without cholangiography), nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration with mediastinal or transthoracic approaches (including sternal split), and other therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth and pharynx. Conversely, a specific extensive lumbar laminectomy with spinal cord or cauda equina exploration and decompression (spanning more than two vertebral segments and without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy) is explicitly excluded from the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 761
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedure is a broad surgical category that falls under the parent grouping “Clinical actions A – G” and comprises a wide array of specific operative techniques. It includes adrenalectomy, partial or complete, or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar or dorsal approaches; cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct and cholangiography; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage or removal of calculi, which may be accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region; exploration of the orbit by a transcranial approach with lesion removal; numerous laminectomy procedures that combine exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord and/or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy or discectomy—ranging from 1‑2 vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis) and thoracic spine to more than two segments in the cervical and thoracic spine; laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and/or excision of a herniated intervertebral disc in single cervical or lumbar interspaces; nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct; and parathyroidectomy or exploration of the parathyroids, including variants with mediastinal exploration performed via cervical, sternal‑split or transthoracic approaches. Some of these methods are listed as possibly included within the exploration category, reflecting flexibility in how they may be combined or selected. Together, these procedures illustrate the diverse yet interrelated nature of exploratory surgeries used for diagnostic assessment and therapeutic intervention across multiple organ systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2167
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that falls under the parent group “Clinical actions A‑G.” It includes a wide range of operative methods such as partial or complete adrenalectomy (with or without biopsy) performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches; cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, both with and without cholangiography; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, optionally combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; transcranial exploration of the orbit with lesion removal; laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and disc excision in a single lumbar interspace; laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration; radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration; and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via cervical or transthoracic approaches. The Excision category explicitly excludes certain laminectomy procedures: lumbar laminectomy with exploration and decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina that involves more than two vertebral segments, and cervical laminectomy with similar exploration that does not include facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. In addition to these operative techniques, Excision is linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx and is related to plastic procedures on the nose. This integrated framework illustrates how Excision groups diverse anatomical surgeries while defining specific procedural exclusions and connections to related therapeutic domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2787
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that serves as the parent concept for Clinical actions A – G and is identified as the answer to the Chinese‑language diagnostic phrase “结直肠癌主要诊断的程序:发现:时间点:^患者:名义型:PhenX,” linking it to colorectal cancer diagnostic processes. Within the Excision hierarchy, a wide array of specific operative methods are enumerated, including Laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord and/or cauda equina (excluding procedures that involve more than two cervical vertebral segments), several variants of cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct (with or without cholangiography, transduodenal sphincterotomy, or sphincteroplasty), Choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage or calculus removal, cranial procedures such as infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy, laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration, nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, and multiple forms of parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration (including approaches with mediastinal exploration, sternal split, or transthoracic access). The category also designates “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx” as a method of Excision and records a relationship to plastic procedures on the nose, indicating its relevance to both therapeutic and reconstructive interventions in the facial region. This integrated view underscores Excision as a versatile procedural framework encompassing diverse operative techniques, specific exclusions, and connections to both diagnostic pathways and plastic surgical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 884
retrieved_docs.content: surgical specialties—biliary, cranial, spinal, urologic, and endocrine—each employing targeted techniques to visualize, assess, and treat pathology while often incorporating adjunctive diagnostic or therapeutic steps such as cholangiography, sphincterotomy, or mediastinal access. This integrated framework aids clinicians in selecting the appropriate exploratory approach based on the anatomical site, underlying condition, and required therapeutic objectives, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 624
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that encompasses a variety of operative techniques across multiple organ systems. Specific procedures that are classified as methods of Excision include partial or complete adrenalectomy or adrenal gland exploration (performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy), arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from a joint, cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty (with optional cholangiography), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, and several forms of parathyroid surgery such as standard parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via a cervical approach, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via a transthoracic approach. Additional therapeutic applications of Excision extend to other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, as well as percutaneous nephrolithotomy. Conversely, certain spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. These excluded methods comprise laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina—without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—performed for spinal stenosis across various spinal regions (sacral, thoracic, cervical, and lumbar) and differing numbers of vertebral segments (one or two segments versus more than two segments). Excision is also linked, albeit in a non‑procedural context, as the answer to a specific colorectal‑cancer diagnostic program entry, and it holds a relationship to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, these connections illustrate that Excision functions as an overarching term for many targeted surgical removals while clearly distinguishing itself from certain decompressive spinal surgeries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2756
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of organ‑specific and region‑specific procedures while explicitly excluding certain extensive spinal decompressions. Within this category, adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, and sometimes accompanied by removal of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—uses an excision method. Cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, either with cholangiography or with choledochoenterostomy, is also classified as an excision technique. Exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region falls under the same umbrella. In the spine, excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated intervertebral disc when performed at a single interspace in the cervical or lumbar spine. By contrast, more extensive laminectomy procedures that decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina over more than two vertebral segments—whether cervical, lumbar, or thoracic—are specifically listed as excluded methods for excision. Other procedures grouped under excision include nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision, parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, and parathyroidectomy that involves mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach. Excision is positioned within the higher‑level grouping “Clinical actions A‑G,” indicating that it is one of the clinical actions encompassed by that classification. Additionally, excision is linked to plastic procedures on the nose and is identified as a method within the broader set of “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx,” reflecting its relevance to reconstructive and therapeutic interventions in those anatomical regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: The community defines Exploration as a broad surgical action within the Clinical actions A–G hierarchy that encompasses organ‑specific investigative and therapeutic operations across endocrine, biliary, breast/genital, neurosurgical, ophthalmic and spinal domains. In the endocrine system Exploration includes adrenalectomy (partial or total) with or without biopsy and possible removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors, as well as parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration that may be extended to mediastinal exploration via cervical, sternal‑split or transthoracic approaches. Biliary Exploration comprises cholecystectomy variants with common‑duct exploration—cholecystectomy with cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy, sphincteroplasty—and choledochotomy or choledochostomy procedures that may involve drainage, calculus removal and adjunctive cholangiography; minimally invasive laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration is also included.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1219
retrieved_docs.content: and orbital procedures listed above. It also encompasses minimally invasive abdominal and thoracic interventions such as nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and a spectrum of parathyroid surgeries—standard parathyroidectomy or direct exploration, and extensions to mediastinal exploration via cervical, sternal‑split, or transthoracic approaches. Excision is further identified as the method for “other or therapeutic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx” and is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, reflecting its role in both functional and aesthetic facial surgery. Importantly, the Excision class explicitly excludes pure laminectomy‑type spinal decompressions that lack facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc excision; these excluded methods cover cervical, lumbar, sacral, and thoracic laminectomies performed on one or two levels as well as multi‑segment laminectomies. This distinction clarifies that such isolated

Conversely, more extensive laminectomies involving multi‑segment exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina—without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—are explicitly excluded from the excision category. Excision further extends to breast, urologic, and endocrine procedures. Nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, radical orchiectomy for tumor removal combined with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy—whether performed with mediastinal exploration via a sternal split or transthoracic approach—are all described as excisional interventions. Beyond these, excision is linked to other therapeutic domains, serving as a method for procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it is associated with plastic procedures on the nose. It also encompasses percutaneous

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712
retrieved_docs.content: Excision serves as a broad parent category for a variety of surgical actions, including the set of procedures labeled “Clinical actions A‑G.” Within this overarching category, many specific operative methods are classified as forms of excision: cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (both open and laparoscopic), choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage or calculus removal (with or without cholecystotomy and with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) that decompress nerve roots and may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc excision, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. At the same time, the excision category explicitly excludes several laminectomy techniques used for spinal stenosis: thoracic laminectomy with cord or cauda equina decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for one or two vertebral segments; and cervical, lumbar, or thoracic laminectomy for more than two vertebral segments performed under the same constraints. Excision is also linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, indicating that excisional techniques extend beyond deep organ surgery to include facial and upper aerodigestive tract interventions. This integrated view highlights excision as a versatile surgical concept that encompasses numerous specific operative methods while delineating clear boundaries for procedures it does not cover.
